text: The patient is positioned supine, then adjusted to reverse Trendelenburg with the left side elevated at a fifteen-degree angle, preparing for the robotic distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy.

Following diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon docks the robotic system and begins the procedure by dividing the gastrocolic ligament with a harmonic scalpel, positioned inferior to the gastroepiploic vessels.
The body of the pancreas is visible through the surgical window, providing a clear view of the anatomical structure during the procedure.
The video shows a remarkably large splenic artery, measuring approximately twelve millimeters in diameter, visibly pulsating in the foreground. The artery follows a serpentine course.
The surgeon continues dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach, reaching the angle of His, completing the mobilization of the stomach.

With the stomach reflected cephalad, the surgeon gains excellent exposure of the surgical field. The peritoneum is incised along the inferior margin of the pancreas, and a medial to lateral dissection is performed.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, dividing the omentum and splenocolic ligament, which results in the full mobilization of the splenic flexure of the colon.
The surgeon switches to a hook cautery to divide the peritoneum at the superior margin of the pancreas, gradually exposing the splenic artery, which begins to come into view.
The surgeon approaches the inferior margin of the pancreas, elevating it from the retroperitoneum to reveal the splenic vein beneath.
The surgeon carries the dissection underneath the splenic vein, separating it from the pancreas. The splenic vein is encircled, and the final dissection of the splenic artery is performed.

The surgeon retracts the splenic artery cephalad, revealing the dorsal pancreatic artery branching inferiorly. Dissection proceeds to the takeoff from the celiac axis. The surgeon navigates underneath the neck of the pancreas, threading a vessel loop through the passageway to prepare for division. The pancreas appears thick despite its normal consistency.

The surgeon attempts to divide the pancreas using a stapler, but the tissue is too thick for the stapler to be effective. After several attempts, the stapler is abandoned, and the surgeon decides to use an energy device to divide the pancreas.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the pancreatic duct. A small arterial bleeder is visible and is controlled using bipolar cautery.
The surgeon identifies the pancreatic duct and suture ligates it with a 3-0 silk suture. The duct is then divided using a harmonic scalpel, exposing the underlying splenic vessels.

The surgeon observes that the splenic artery is larger than the vein in this patient. The artery is divided first using a tan load of staples, followed by the division of the splenic vein with the same staple load.

The surgeon begins the retroperitoneal dissection, elevating the pancreas from the retroperitoneum. Adhesions are visible between the tumor and the transverse mesocolon, and a portion of the mesocolon is taken with the specimen.
The surgeon begins to maneuver past the tumor, preparing to take down the peritoneal reflection.

The surgeon takes down the final aspect of the peritoneal reflection supporting the spleen, freeing the specimen. The pancreatic bed is irrigated and inspected for hemostasis. Three polypropylene mattress sutures are placed across the pancreas.
The surgeon places a drain along the pancreatic bed and retrieves the specimen using a large container through the supraumbilical site.
